command: dd if=/dev/sda of=/var/tmp/system_current.img bs=4M status=progress && sha256sum /var/tmp/system_current.img | awk '{print $1 "  /var/tmp/system_current.img"}' > /var/tmp/system_current.sha256 && diff -u baseline/system.sha256 /var/tmp/system_current.sha256
explanation: Creates a full disk image snapshot and compares its SHA-256 signature line against a stored baseline signature to detect any unauthorized system image changes.
rationale_steps: Use dd to image the entire disk to a temporary file.
Compute and format the SHA-256 signature line for the captured image.
Diff the signature line against a baseline signature file to confirm integrity.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed Bash pipeline with valid dd, sha256sum, awk, and diff usage, balanced quoting, and correct operators.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It implements a plausible integrity-check to detect system image tampering (relevant to T1601 detection), but it does not modify the image and typically requires root to read /dev/sda despite the 'Privilege: None' claim.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed with proper use of &&, pipe operators, balanced quoting, valid flags for dd (if, of, bs, status), sha256sum, awk, and diff (-u), with consistent file path references throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements a defensive integrity-checking workflow aligned with T1601 detection mitigations—imaging the disk with dd, computing a SHA-256 checksum, and diffing against a baseline—though it requires root privileges to read /dev/sda (contradicting the 'None' privilege claim), and comparing only the checksum line rather than the full image hash file is a minor realism gap.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command sequence uses correct bash syntax, valid flags for all utilities, and proper piping and redirection.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command requires root privileges to read the raw device (despite the metadata stating None), and imaging a live system to its own partition will cause checksum mismatches and potential disk exhaustion.
